goal_09.config.title: The unfinished digital revolution: expanding internet access
goal_09.config.abstract: Internet access has grown at remarkable speed, yet digital opportunity remains deeply unequal. While networks now reach much of the world, millions of people, especially in poorer countries, are still shut out of the digital economy. The reasons go beyond infrastructure, and reflect gaps in affordability, skills, and inclusive policy design.
goal_09.config.citation: Pirlea, Ana Florina and Lorena Corso. 2026. “The unfinished digital revolution: expanding internet access” In Atlas of Global Development 2026, edited by A. F. Pirlea, D. Wadhwa, D. Mahler, U. Serajuddin, A. Thudt, and M. Welch. Washington, DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO. https://www.doi.org/10.60616/pp3f-d004
goal_09.config.homeFactoidText: people are not using the internet
goal_09.config.homeTagline: Internet access has grown at remarkable speed since the turn of the century, yet millions of people, especially in poorer countries, are still shut out of the digital economy.
goal_09.content.#0.text: Mind the gap: the global connection deficit
goal_09.content.#1.text: Internet access lowers the cost of economic and social transactions, making workers more productive and enabling innovation, trade, and job creation. But access alone is not enough. Without supportive policies around competition, skills, and strong institutions, digital adoption may fail to generate broad‑based gains and can even widen inequality.[reference: int1]
goal_09.content.#2.text: How Many People Are Still Offline?
goal_09.content.#3.text: Although internet access has expanded quickly, [emphasis: over a quarter of the world’s population remains unconnected].
goal_09.content.#4.label: people are still offline
goal_09.content.#5.text: The share of people using the internet is measured primarily through household surveys: individuals are asked whether they used the Internet in the previous three months, from any device (computer, phone, TV, etc.) and any location (home, work, school, café).[footnote: If no recent survey exists, statistical modelling is used to estimate values based on correlates like mobile-subscriptions, GDP per capita, education, and regional patterns.] [reference: intm1]
goal_09.content.#6.title: Global Internet access: trends and divides
goal_09.content.#6.visdescription: A scroller showing three scenes, each of them a line chart, showing the rise in internet access between 2005 and 2025 for the world, regional groupings, and income groups
goal_09.content.#6.source: International Telecommunications Union (ITU). Retrieved from World Development Indicators (IT.NET.USER.ZS)
goal_09.content.#6.keyFactLabel: people are not using the internet
goal_09.content.#6.scenes.#0.text: Worldwide, the share of people who access the internet has increased from 16 percent only two decades ago to over 70 percent today. Still, over a quarter of the global population (2.2 billion) remains offline.
goal_09.content.#6.scenes.#1.text: Internet adoption mirrors a country’s level of development: nearly universal in high-income nations (94 percent) but less than a quarter (23 percent) in low-income countries.
goal_09.content.#6.scenes.#2.text: Basic internet access is lowest in Sub-Saharan Africa, where only around a third (36 percent) of the population is online.
goal_09.content.#7.text: Who is left behind? Women and rural areas
goal_09.content.#8.text: Rural areas, in both high and low income economies, often lag behind urban centers when it comes to internet access, reflecting gaps in infrastructure and income.[emphasis: In rural settings, limited][emphasis: internet access constrains the use of digital services], such as market information (including prices), agricultural support (weather data for farmers), digital payments, remote health services, and access to government programs.[reference: rur1] This disparity is especially pronounced in the rural areas of the poorest countries, where only 14 percent of people use the internet; even in lower middle income countries around a half of the people in rural areas are without internet access.
goal_09.content.#9.title: The urban-rural digital divide
goal_09.content.#9.subtitle: Individuals using the Internet (2025)
goal_09.content.#9.visdescription: A panel of four line charts, each showing the level of internet access for urban and rural areas between 2022 and 2025, for each income region. The gap is represented by a shaded area between each pair of lines.
goal_09.content.#10.text: Another critical divide when it comes to internet access is the [emphasis: gender gap]. Women and girls are less likely to use the internet regularly, especially in low-income economies where about 30 percent of men but less than 20 percent of women are online..[reference: gnd2]
goal_09.content.#12.text: There were [emphasis: 280 million more men than women using the Internet ]in 2025.[reference: gnd1]
goal_09.content.#13.text: The high cost of mobile devices, limited literacy and digital skills, and in some cases low awareness of mobile internet are several persistent challenges which limit internet use. While these are faced by both men and women, [emphasis: women are oftentimes more affected than men by barriers to internet use due to structural inequalities in income, education, and employment], which make devices less affordable and digital skills harder to acquire. In some settings, restrictive social norms and lack of family approval further limit women’s ability to access and use mobile internet. Moreover, some internet activities, including social media, may carry safety and health risks for youth, especially young women.[reference: gnd4] [reference: gnd5] [reference: gnd6] [reference: gnd3] These gender disparities exclude women from many economic, educational, and social opportunities offered by digital technologies.
goal_09.content.#14.title: Gender gaps in internet access are wide in low- and lower-middle income economies
goal_09.content.#14.subtitle: Individuals using the Internet (% of population), (male and female), 2025
goal_09.content.#14.source: International Telecommunication Union, ITU DataHub. Retrieved from World Development Indicators (IT.NET.USER.MA.ZS, IT.NET.USER.FE.ZS),
goal_09.content.#14.visdescription: Bar chart showing the internet access levels for men and women, by income group
goal_09.content.#16.text: Expanding network coverage and internet usage, especially in poorer countries, is also one of the key targets under the Sustainable Development Goals framework (targets 9.c, 17.6 and 17.8).
goal_09.content.#17.text: Currently, high levels of internet access are concentrated in rich economies. However, despite significant infrastructure costs, as well as affordability and digital literacy barriers, over the past decade most countries have managed to steadily increase internet access. Notably, several lower-middle-income economies have emerged as strong performers when it comes to expanding  access to the internet.
goal_09.content.#18.text: The chart below shows, by country, the change in internet access levels between 2015 and 2023. It also depicts, using transparent arrows, where the countries would have been, if they had followed the typical pace of progress since 2015 (see more details about how this is calculated here). This allows us to see which countries have progressed faster than expected, considering their access levels in 2015.  You can explore further here.
goal_09.content.#19.title: Almost all countries have managed to steadily increase internet access
goal_09.content.#19.subtitle: Individuals using the Internet (% of population)
goal_09.content.#19.source: International Telecommunication Union, ITU DataHub and World Bank staff calculations, based on XXXX 2026 (to be added when working paper available)
goal_09.content.#19.visdescription: This chart shows, by country, the internet access levels in 2015 and 2024, using arrows. It also shows where the countries would have been, if they had followed the typical pace of progress since 2015.
goal_09.content.#19.scenes.#0.text: By 2024, in 54 countries more than 90 percent of the population was using the internet.
goal_09.content.#19.scenes.#1.text: Countries like [emphasis: Cambodia ]and the[emphasis:  Kyrgyz Republic ]have made much more progress by 2024 in increasing internet usage than would have been expected based on their access levels in 2015. In Cambodia, internet access levels rose from less than 7 percent to close to 70 percent of the population. The Kyrgyz Republic also had a remarkable performance, moving from 30 percent of the population accessing the internet in 2015 to over 90 percent by 2024.
goal_09.content.#20.text: Fixed vs. mobile broadband: complementary pathways
goal_09.content.#21.text: Globally, measuring internet use takes into account any online activity, even those requiring minimal data, such as emailing or browsing. However, data-intensive services, like remote learning or telemedicine, or the integration of Artificial Intelligence into daily life[footnote: Also see the discussion on Inequalities in AI use and exposure], demand high-speed access or [emphasis: broadband]. There are two primary types of broadband connections: fixed and mobile.
goal_09.content.#22.text: Fixed and mobile broadband differ in how they are delivered and in the trade-offs they offer for users. [emphasis: Fixed broadband] typically provides faster, more reliable connections but depends on extensive, costly infrastructure and is therefore concentrated in denser, higher-income areas. [emphasis: Mobile broadband], by contrast, is delivered wirelessly, making it cheaper to deploy and more flexible, though often with more variable performance.
goal_09.content.#23.text: The illustration below summarizes the key differences between these two technologies.
goal_09.content.#24.visdescription: The illustration shows the main differences between fixed and mobile broadband. Fixed broadband: wired connection (fiber, cable, digital line subscriber); home & business use; high and consistent speeds; typically high or unlimited data; line installation required. Mobile broadband: wireless connection (3G/4G/5G); on-the-go and portable; variable speeds and performance; usage based or capped data; SIM/eSIM access
goal_09.content.#25.text: In practice, the two technologies are complementary: fixed broadband underpins high-capacity national networks and enterprise use, while mobile broadband drives widespread personal connectivity and inclusion.
goal_09.content.#26.text: [emphasis: Fixed broadband connections are common in many high-income countries], [emphasis: but nearly absent in low-income economies]. Extending fixed broadband to rural, remote, or low-income areas is often commercially unviable: infrastructure works like laying fiber-optic cables underground are expensive, while revenues remain low due to sparse populations and limited purchasing power. This mismatch between high upfront investment and low returns discourages private operators without public support or cost-sharing. [reference: brdb1][emphasis: The majority of people who use the internet do so using mobile broadband, ]especially in lower-income economies, as it is cheaper and faster to deploy.
goal_09.content.#27.title: Worldwide, mobile broadband far surpasses fixed broadband adoption
goal_09.content.#27.source: International Telecommunication Union, ITU DataHub. Retrieved from Data360 (WB_WDI_IT_NET_BBND_P2, ITU_DH_ACT_MOB_PER_100)
goal_09.content.#27.visdescription: Scatterplot showing the number of fixed broadband and mobile broadband subscriptions, per 100 people, by country
goal_09.content.#27.scenes.#0.text: Between 2015 and 2025, fixed broadband levels doubled, reaching 20 subscriptions per 100 people. Meanwhile, [emphasis: mobile internet connectivity has surged to 99 active mobile broadband subscriptions per 100 people.]
goal_09.content.#27.scenes.#1.text: Fixed broadband is common in many high-income countries. In France and Germany, for example, there are approximately 49 and 46 fixed broadband connections per 100 people. China, an upper middle income country, is also highly connected, with 47 subscriptions to fixed high-speed access per 100 people (as of 2024).
goal_09.content.#27.scenes.#2.text: However, fixed broadband is nearly absent in low-income economies. In [emphasis: Côte d’Ivoire ]and[emphasis:  Eswatini], fixed broadband penetration is minimal: there are 2 and 2.5 fixed broadband subscriptions per 100 people.
goal_09.content.#27.scenes.#3.text: [emphasis: Across much of the world, people connect to the internet through mobile phones]. In Côte d’Ivoire and in Eswatini there are 108 and 116 active mobile broadband subscriptions, respectively, per 100 people. (In many countries, there are more than 100 mobile broadband subscriptions per 100 people because individuals and businesses maintain multiple connections, for example for smartphones, tablets, work devices, or other connected equipment.[reference: mbrs1])
goal_09.content.#29.text: Globally, most internet users use their mobile phones and mobile broadband to go online. Mobile broadband provides wireless internet access via cellular networks (mostly through 4G or 5G) using SIM-based devices (like cell phones) or fixed-wireless terminals.[emphasis: ]
goal_09.content.#30.text: [emphasis: Mobile networks have advanced rapidly over the past three decades], from basic digital communication to highly sophisticated connectivity. Second-generation (2G/GSM) networks in the 1990s marked the shift from analog (similar to traditional radio broadcast, with no encryption) to digital (information converted into digital data and encrypted before transmission). This improved voice quality and enabled text messaging. Third-generation (3G) technology brought mobile internet into everyday use, making web browsing and video calls widely accessible. Fourth-generation (4G/LTE) networks have delivered broadband-like speeds, supporting high definition video and today’s data‑intensive apps. [reference: netc1]
goal_09.content.#31.alt: An illustration showing the main capabilities for each type of mobile network technology: 1G (calls), 2G (calls and text), 3G (calls, text, internet browsing), 4G (calls, text, internet browsing, high- quality video calls), 5G (all the capabilities of 4G and the Internet of things)
goal_09.content.#33.text: [emphasis: The latest generation of mobile networks (5G) is designed to transmit data very quickly.] This makes it possible for large numbers of everyday objects, such as sensors and appliances to be connected and to communicate continuously (a concept known as the “Internet of Things”).[reference: iot1] This capability supports better public services and the development of smart cities (for example, allowing traffic lights to adjust to congestion).[reference: iot2] The use of 5G enables ambulances to transmit real-time patient data and video to hospitals, improving triage and reducing treatment delays.[reference: 5gneth]
goal_09.content.#34.title: Most of the world is covered by 3G and 4G networks. Sub-Saharan Africa is the least connected region
goal_09.content.#34.subtitle: Percentage of inhabitants that are within range of at least a 3G, 4G, or 5G mobile-cellular signal, irrespective of whether or not they are subscribers
goal_09.content.#34.source: International Telecommunication Union, ITU DataHub. Retrieved from Data360 (ITU_DH_POP_COV_3G, ITU_DH_POP_COV_4G, ITU_DH_POP_COV_5G)
goal_09.content.#34.visdescription: The visualisation shows three choropleth world maps with the level of country coverage for at least 3G, 4G, and 5G networks
goal_09.content.#34.keyFactLabel: people living in Sub-Saharan Africa are not covered by a 5G network
goal_09.content.#34.scenes.#0.text: Currently, 96 percent of the global population lives within reach of at least a  3G network.
goal_09.content.#34.scenes.#1.text: Although 4G networks cover over 93 percent of the people in the world, [emphasis: low income countries, mostly in Sub-Saharan Africa, still face stark gaps in 4G coverage], with rural areas especially underserved.
goal_09.content.#34.scenes.#2.text: 5G is widely available in many high-income economies. In some upper and lower-middle-income countries like China, India, or Thailand over 80 percent of people are also covered by 5G networks.
goal_09.content.#34.scenes.#3.text: In Sub-Saharan Africa, the deployment of 5G remains largely confined to a few high-density urban areas, and only [emphasis: Mauritius ]and [emphasis: South Africa ]have coverage rates exceeding 50 percent of the population.
goal_09.content.#35.text: Expanding mobile network coverage in low income countries is challenging due to high infrastructure costs and weak supporting systems. Although not as costly as introducing fixed broadband, mobile broadband still requires significant investments.
goal_09.content.#36.text: Extending mobile coverage to rural and remote areas is often not commercially viable without government support. Unlike urban areas, where towers, fiber backhaul, power, and rights of way already exist, rural areas in many cases lack this infrastructure and require costly new construction, frequently over difficult terrain. Lower population density and incomes limit revenues, while unreliable power, often relying on diesel or off‑grid solutions, further raises capital and maintenance costs. [reference: infr1] [reference: rurinf1] [reference: infr3]
goal_09.content.#37.text: Internet access is the first step toward participating in the digital world. Yet once people are online, connection speed determines which activities are possible and how much those connections translate into real opportunities and improved well‑being.
goal_09.content.#39.text: Internet speed heavily impacts the quality of online experiences. [emphasis: Fast broadband speed supports economic growth, productivity and innovation, and allows access to better education and healthcare services.] [reference: spd1] [reference: spd2] [reference: spd3] In countries like Singapore, Chile, or United Arab Emirates the median download speeds for either mobile or fixed broadband, or both, are high and supportive of a wide range of activities (over 300 Mbps), while in some low-income economies, median internet connections are often much slower (under 20 Mbps).
goal_09.content.#40.title: High income countries enjoy high speed internet
goal_09.content.#40.subtitle: Median country download speeds (mbps)
goal_09.content.#40.visdescription: The visual is a dot chart race that is meant to illustrate the median download speed by country for fixed and mobile broadband
goal_09.content.#41.text: From coverage to connection: the usage gap
goal_09.content.#42.text: There is often a significant gap between network coverage and actual internet use.[emphasis: Even when broadband networks reach most of the population, many people remain offline due to high costs for devices and data packages][footnote: Also see the discussion on the affordability of data packages here.][emphasis: as well as low levels of digital literacy.] For example, in Bangladesh, 4G mobile networks covered 100 percent of the population, yet only 53 percent of people were observed to be using the internet (as of 2024).
goal_09.content.#43.label: covered by a network are not online
goal_09.content.#44.text: This disconnect also shows that infrastructure alone is not enough to achieve true digital inclusion. Understanding the reasons why people remain unconnected, even when network coverage is available, is essential for designing effective and equitable interventions that facilitate meaningful access and use.
goal_09.content.#45.title: Mobile networks now reach most of the population, but many people remain offline
goal_09.content.#45.source: International Telecommunication Union, ITU DataHub. Retrieved from Data360 (ITU_DH_POP_COV_3G, WB_WDI_IT_NET_USER_ZS)
goal_09.content.#45.visdescription: The visualization is a set of four slope chart showing 3G network coverage and internet access levels for countries, by income group: low, lower middle, upper middle, and high income
goal_09.content.#46.text: Digital literacy: the key to meaningful access
goal_09.content.#47.text: Digital literacy is essential for meaningful internet use. [emphasis: Many people lack the skills needed to navigate online resources and to communicate through mobile devices], underscoring the importance of education and training initiatives. In some countries, more than half of the population has never sent a text message using a mobile phone.[emphasis:  ]This gap in technology usage  illustrates how limited digital skills can prevent individuals from benefiting fully from available connectivity.
goal_09.content.#48.title: In 27 countries more the 50% of people have never sent a text message
goal_09.content.#48.subtitle: Share of respondents who report that they have never sent a text message through a mobile phone (SMS, text message or through a messaging app)
goal_09.content.#48.source: The Global Findex Database (2025), indicator: [con16] "Can send a text message on a mobile phone (%, age 15+)". The data shows the percentage of respondents who report that they sent a text message through a mobile phone. This can include an SMS or text message or a message on a messaging app, such as WhatsApp.
goal_09.content.#50.text: One of the biggest reasons many communities remain offline is simply the cost of devices such as smartphones and computers. The World Bank’s 2025 FINDEX report shows that not having enough money to buy a smartphone is the leading barrier to owning one, and without a smartphone people cannot use the internet. [emphasis: The device cost barrier is even more acute for adults in the poorest 40 percent of the population.] In some countries in Sub-Saharan Africa, the average cost of a smartphone can represent almost 40 percent of an adult’s monthly income [reference: devaf1], and about 53 percent of the monthly income in South Asia.[reference: devaf2] Improving access to affordable devices is not just helpful but essential for real digital inclusion.
goal_09.content.#51.title: Few people in low income countries have smartphones
goal_09.content.#51.subtitle: The share of people who report that their main mobile phone is a smartphone
goal_09.content.#51.source: The Global Findex Database (2025), indicators: [con9a] “Main mobile phone is a smartphone (%, age 15+)”
goal_09.content.#51.visdescription: Vertical dot plot which shows the share of people who report that their main mobile phone is a smartphone by country and income group
goal_09.content.#51.mobile_not_main: Share of people not owning a smartphone
goal_09.content.#51.lack_of_money: Lack of money as a reason for having no smartphone
goal_09.content.#51.keyFactLabel: of people in low income countries own a smartphone
goal_09.content.#51.scenes.#0.text: [emphasis: The share of people who do not have a smartphone remains high in poorer countries], reaching nearly 80 percent in low-income economies and about 50 percent in lower-middle-income economies.
goal_09.content.#51.scenes.#1.text: In several countries, including [emphasis: in Malawi, ][emphasis: Madagascar][emphasis: , and Chad, fewer than 14 percent of adults own a smartphone]. These patterns highlight the strong association between income levels and smartphone ownership, and point to the persistent barriers faced by the poorest population groups in accessing more advanced mobile technologies.
goal_09.content.#52.text: Internet access opens the door to better jobs, more productive businesses, improved public services, and new ways to learn, connect, and innovate. Being connected makes it easier to learn new skills, find employment, access education and health services, and participate more fully in social and civic life. These opportunities will continue to expand as digital technologies, including artificial intelligence, become more embedded in everyday life. Yet extending those benefits to everyone will require more than expanding networks. Affordability, digital skills, and inclusive policies remain critical to increasing meaningful internet use, especially for poorer, rural, and marginalized communities. Encouragingly, many countries have demonstrated that with sustained investment and the right policy mix, digital gaps can narrow. Building on this momentum can help make digital expansion more productive and inclusive.

goal_09.references.mrbs1: International Telecommunication Union. 5G subscriptions already represent more than one-third of all mobile broadband subscriptions
